turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Ginsburg has the opinion of the Court in two cases this morning.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: The first case is BNSF Railway Co. v. Loos, number 17-1042. Respondent Michael Loos was injured while working at petitioner BNSF Railway Company?s rail yard. Loos sued BNSF under the Federal Employers Liability Act (FELA), gaining a jury verdict of more than $100,000. Of that amount, the jury ascribed $30,000 to wages lost during the time Loos was unable to work. BNSF moved for an offset against the judgment. The lost wages awarded Loos, BNSF maintained, ranked as compensation taxable under the Railroad Retirement Tax Act (RRTA). The railway therefore urged that it had to withhold part of the $3,000 attributable to lost wages to cover Loos' share of RRTA taxes. The District Court and the Court of Appeals for the Eighth Circuit rejected the requested offset holding that Loos' lost wages were not subject to RRTA taxation. The question presented is a railroad's payment to an employee for working time lost due to an on-the-job injury taxable compensation under the RRTA? We granted review to resolve a division of opinion on the answer to that question and now hold that lost wages recovered by a railroad worker are taxable compensation. In 1937, Congress adopted a self-sustaining retirement benefits system for railroad workers. The Railroad Retirement Act (RRA) accords railroad workers various benefits. The RRTA funds those benefits by imposing a payroll tax on both railroads and their employees. Benefits under the RRA and taxes under the RRTA are measured by the employee's compensation, a term both statutes define as any form of money remuneration paid to an individual for services rendered as an employee. The Railroad Retirement System mirrors the Social Security scheme. For Social Security purposes tax and benefit amounts are determined by the worker's wages. As defined in the Social Security Act, wages mean all remuneration for any service of whatever manner performed by an employee. This definition of the term wages bears a striking resemblance to the RRTA's definition of the term compensation. Given this resemblance, our decisions on the meaning of wages for Social Security purposes in two cases, Social Security Board v. Nierotko and the United States v. Quality Stores inform our understanding of the RRTA term compensation. In Nierotko, we held that wages embrace both pay for active service and pay received for periods of absence from active service and we determined specifically that that pay for time lost due to the employer's wrong counted as wages. In Quality Stores, we held that severance payments qualified as wages. In line with these decisions, we hold today that compensation under the RRTA encompasses, in addition to pay for active service, pay for periods of absence from active service if the remuneration in question stems from the employer-employee relationship. Damages for lost wages attributable to the railroad's negligence fit comfortably within this invasive definition. The Eighth Circuit's construction of compensation for RRTA purposes to mean only pay for active service is at odds with Nierotko and Quality Stores. In addition, the RRTA contains pinpointed exclusions from taxable compensation for certain types of payments for time lost. These express exclusions signal that non-excluded pay for time lost remains RRTA taxable compensation. Loos argues that the exclusion of personal injury damages from gross income for federal income tax purposes should carry over to the RRTA's tax on income of railroad workers. But Congress specified employee compensation as the RRTA tax base, not gross income and Congress did not exclude personal injury damages from compensation. For the reasons just summarized, stated more completely in the Court's opinion, we reverse the judgment of the Court of Appeals. Justice Gorsuch has filed a dissenting opinion, joined by Justice Thomas.
